our_argument_summary: Our review argues that the pre-1990 NMP linear drift is non-discriminating: both models fit the same NOAA data, and the dome provides no distinct mechanism or predicted drift rate. We cite 10–15 km/yr before 1990 (up to 15–18 km/yr in the 1975–1990 decade). We also note the dome's claimed 0.0466 deg/yr rate cannot be reproduced from NOAA NP.xy by any standard metric. The verdict text adds that the dome's metric is undefined, rendering the claimed rate unverifiable.

relevant_files: monitor.py — NMP Longitude domain, NMP Drift Rate domain (both fetch NOAA NP.xy)
inject_ai_layer.py — WIN-006 listed as confirmed, 0.0466 deg/yr hardcoded
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py fetches NP.xy from https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/data/poles/NP.xy, parses (lon, lat, year) tuples, tests: (1) current NMP longitude > 130°E, (2) current drift rate vs formula 55×exp(-0.08×(year-2015)) km/yr. NEITHER domain validates the pre-1990 linearity claim that is WIN-006's core assertion. The 'confirmed' status comes from inject_ai_layer.py's hardcoded assertion, not from any live computation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The 0.0466 deg/yr figure and 'confirmed' status are hardcoded text in inject_ai_layer.py. No script computes a linear regression on 1590-1990 NP.xy data. The live-fetched NMP domains test current position/velocity only.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: NMP Drift Rate domain uses an adaptive tolerance with 50% floor. For 2025, model predicts 24.7 km/yr vs observed ~40 km/yr (38% error) — still passes under 50% tolerance. The explain_failure function pre-writes its own excuse: 'Deceleration constant (0.08) may need refinement to ~0.065.'
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None identified. Our review correctly states the dome offers no equations or predicted drift rate for the pre-1990 period, and the code confirms no pre-1990 validation routine exists.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py fetches NP.xy for two NMP-related domains (NMP Longitude, NMP Drift Rate). However, neither domain validates WIN-006's specific claim of pre-1990 linear drift at 0.0466 deg/yr — they only test current position and drift rate. Tagging as live_fetch because a related NMP domain does exist and does fetch live data, even though the specific linearity claim is validated only by hardcoded assertion in inject_ai_layer.py. This is a gray area — could arguably be 'hardcoded' since the WIN-006-specific confirmation is hardcoded.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard geophysics has documented NMP drift since the 1600s. The dome model renames the underlying mechanism from 'core-mantle boundary convection' to 'aetheric vortex dynamics' without changing any numerical prediction. Livermore & Finlay (2020, Nature Geoscience) provide specific physical mechanism (competing flux lobes under Canada and Siberia).
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: NOAA NP.xy data has been publicly available for decades. The dome model adopted the known observation (NMP drift trajectory 1590-1990) as a 'prediction' after the data was already published. No evidence the dome model predicted any drift rate before seeing the NOAA data.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome site says 'aetheric vortex has threshold crossing behavior — below threshold: slow linear precession' but provides no derivation from dome geometry (disc_radius, firmament height, etc.) that yields 0.0466 deg/yr or any other rate. The claimed rate is simply the NOAA data restated.

holes_found.description: The monitoring tag 'live_fetch' for WIN-006 is technically defensible but somewhat misleading. The NMP domains in monitor.py fetch live NOAA data, but they test current position/drift — not the pre-1990 linearity claim. The WIN-006 'confirmed' status actually comes from a hardcoded assertion in inject_ai_layer.py. This nuance could be noted more explicitly in our code analysis discussion if WIN-006 is ever cited as a live_fetch example.
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed to the tag (it follows the convention that any NMP-related domain counts), but if Part 4.6 ever cites WIN-006 specifically, clarify that the live fetch validates current NMP position, not the historical linearity claim.

holes_found.description: The dome's source text (02-wins.txt line 20-22) claims the 'aetheric vortex has a threshold crossing behavior' that predicts the pre-1990/post-1990 contrast. Our review could be slightly stronger by explicitly quoting this claim and noting it provides no mathematical framework: no threshold value, no crossing time prediction, no rate equation. Currently we say 'no equations, no predicted drift rate' which is correct but doesn't directly engage with the dome's 'threshold crossing' language.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding a brief phrase noting the dome claims 'threshold crossing behavior' for the NMP but specifies no threshold value, no crossing time, and no mechanism by which a sub-threshold vortex would produce specifically linear drift.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's source text (02-wins.txt) uses the phrase 'slow linear drift (~0.05 deg/year for 400 years)' — note the '~0.05' rounds UP from 0.0466, adding another layer of imprecision to an already non-reproducible number. The dome can't even cite its own claimed rate consistently (0.0466 on the WIN list, ~0.05 in the narrative). This internal inconsistency strengthens our 'unverifiable metric' argument.

kernel_of_truth.description: The pre-1990/post-1990 NMP contrast is genuinely scientifically significant. The pole's behavior did change character around 1990, from relatively slow drift (~10-15 km/yr) to rapid directed motion toward Siberia (peaking ~55 km/yr around 2005). The dome model correctly identifies this as notable.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The standard model provides a specific, testable explanation: Livermore & Finlay (2020) identified competing magnetic flux lobes at the core-mantle boundary that explain both the pre-1990 stability and the post-1990 acceleration. The dome's 'aetheric vortex threshold' provides no equations, no predicted timing, no predicted rate, and no testable distinction from the standard explanation. Noting a real phenomenon doesn't constitute a prediction.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 review of WIN-006. Both Cycle 1 recommendations were successfully applied:

1. APPLIED: 'roughly 9 km per year' was updated to 'roughly 10–15 km per year before 1990 (NOAA historical records; rates reached ~15–18 km/yr in the 1975–1990 decade)' — matching published literature.

2. APPLIED: The dome's 0.0466 deg/yr non-reproducibility was added to detail_verdict_text with specific alternative metrics (great-circle: ~0.010–0.012 deg/yr; longitude regression: ~0.038 deg/yr).

The verdict 'Std Model Explains' remains correct. The evidence text accurately describes this as non-discriminating — both models fit the same NOAA data, the dome offers no distinct mechanism, and the claimed rate is unverifiable.

Code analysis tags re-validated:
- monitoring: 'live_fetch' — technically correct (NMP domains in monitor.py do fetch NOAA NP.xy), though the fetched data tests current position/drift, not the pre-1990 linearity claim. The WIN-006-specific 'confirmed' status comes from inject_ai_layer.py, not from live computation. This is a known gray area but consistent with how other NMP-related WINs are tagged.
- relabels_standard: true — correct. Core-mantle boundary convection → 'aetheric vortex dynamics' with no changed numerical predictions.
- post_hoc: true — correct. NOAA NP.xy data long predates the dome model.
- derives_from_dome: false — correct. No derivation from dome geometry yields 0.0466 deg/yr or any drift rate.

New finding this cycle: The dome's own source text inconsistently rounds the rate to '~0.05 deg/year' in the narrative (02-wins.txt line 20) while citing '0.0466 deg/year' in the WIN list (line 69). This internal inconsistency adds to the 'unverifiable metric' argument but is minor.

Overall assessment: WIN-006's review text is now solid. The Cycle 1 fixes addressed the two main weaknesses. No critical or major issues remain. Two minor suggestions for potential future polish but no recommended action needed.

citations_unchecked: Livermore & Finlay (2020) — referenced in our analysis but not cited in WIN-006's HTML text directly; the WIN-006 detail_evidence references 'NOAA historical records' and 'NOAA NP.xy dataset' only
